coredata.dc:description: Chinese herbal has been widely researched towards the treatment of disease in recent years. These natural products and the derivatives have demonstrated significant value with variety of pharmacodynamical effects. In this work, aiming to explore the application scenarios of Chinese herbal and enrich its functions, we developed the bottom-up synthetic strategy and prepared series of functional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based on curcumin which extracted from Curcuma longa Linn. Cellular assays, including CCK8, transwell migration, invasion, and apoptotic assays, were performed to assess the biological effects of CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4. A xenograft mouse model was established to evaluate the effectivities of tumor inhibition in vivo. RNA sequencing was used to elucidate the molecular regulatory mechanisms of CCQDs. As expected, the carbonyl groups on curcumin facilitated the intermolecular cross-linking with amino-rich monomers by Schiff base reaction. Compared to the original curcumin, all of the curcumin-derived CQDs (CCQDs) possessed superior solubility in water and advanced biocompatibility, while inherited the fluorescent properties of CQDs and the apoptosis towards tumor cells of curcumin. Notably, the CCQDs could efficiently enter cells while stain and label the cytoplasm. Both in vitro and in vivo results confirmed that CCQDs could significantly eliminate tumor cells and inhibit the tumor growth. This work provided a successful showcase of modification and functionalization of Chinses herbal and highlighted the curcumin as a promising candidate for cancer theranostics.

head.ce:title: Curcumin derived functional carbon quantum dots for enhanced tumor theranostic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chinese herbal has been widely researched towards the treatment of disease in recent years. These natural products and the derivatives have demonstrated significant value with variety of pharmacodynamical effects. In this work, aiming to explore the application scenarios of Chinese herbal and enrich its functions, we developed the bottom-up synthetic strategy and prepared series of functional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based on curcumin which extracted from Curcuma longa Linn. Cellular assays, including CCK8, transwell migration, invasion, and apoptotic assays, were performed to assess the biological effects of CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4. A xenograft mouse model was established to evaluate the effectivities of tumor inhibition in vivo. RNA sequencing was used to elucidate the molecular regulatory mechanisms of CCQDs. As expected, the carbonyl groups on curcumin facilitated the intermolecular cross-linking with amino-rich monomers by Schiff base reaction. Compared to the original curcumin, all of the curcumin-derived CQDs (CCQDs) possessed superior solubility in water and advanced biocompatibility, while inherited the fluorescent properties of CQDs and the apoptosis towards tumor cells of curcumin. Notably, the CCQDs could efficiently enter cells while stain and label the cytoplasm. Both in vitro and in vivo results confirmed that CCQDs could significantly eliminate tumor cells and inhibit the tumor growth. This work provided a successful showcase of modification and functionalization of Chinses herbal and highlighted the curcumin as a promising candidate for cancer theranostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional Chinese medicine, as a treasure of the ancient Chinese science, has emerged obvious advantages to prevent or treat diseases and been widely researched in recent years. Chinese herbal as the important part of traditional Chinese medical and clinical process is of significant value and plays a variety of pharmacodynamical effects for thousands of years [1]. Chinese herbal such as artemisinin has attracted numerous research attentions [2]. More and more Clinical practices have proved that Chinese herbal could exhibit unique effects towards special disease [3,4]. Meanwhile, many drugs such as paclitaxel and vincristine that derived from natural products have shown very clear anti-tumor effects. Among them, curcumin has been evidenced with strong anti-inflammatory effect and proved to induce cell apoptosis, inhibiting multiple cells signaling pathways towards tumor proliferation and invasion [5–9]. Especially, even for the lung cancer which is one of the most common and lethal cancers, curcumin could enhance the sensitivity of lung cancer cells to chemotherapy through dual induction of autophagy and apoptosis, as well as regulate the aging and proliferation of fibroblasts. Recently, it has also been reported that curcumin could induce ferroptosis of tumor cells through autophagy [10,11]. Moreover, similar to most Chinese herbal medicines that are from roots, leaves or stems of plants, the usage of curcumin which extracted from Curcuma longa Linn. holds the capability to scale up conveniently, demonstrating great superiorities from the industrial and economic point of view [12]. Unfortunately, suffering from the insolubility in water and the rather low utilization rate of oral administration, the efficacy of curcumin is limited and the medicinal value cannot be fully reflected [13]. which are also regarded as the common obstacles and have puzzled the application of most traditional Chinese herbal. Hence, it is vital to analyze the active components, study the related chemical structures, and develop the modification and functionalization of Chinese herbal.

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the emergent class of carbonaceous materials with high biocompatibility have gained significant attention in the field of nanomedicine including tumor diagnosis and therapy [14–20]. Due to the unique features such as small particle size, tailorable photoluminescence properties, and multiple surface functionalization, CQDs have already been widely explored in bioimaging and biosensing, drug delivery as well as optoelectronic devices [21–24]. The nanoscale dimensions and adjustable surface chemical properties have evidenced the capability to cross the blood–brain barrier and facilitated the cell endocytosis [25–27]. Remarkably, when used as fluorescent probes to visualize the drug accumulation and activities at pathological sites, CQDs demonstrate more desirable properties with superior stability and outstanding photoluminescence property compared to more traditional markers [24,28–31]. CQDs based drug carriers also contain promoted solubility, biocompatibility and cellular uptake ratio. Thereby, benefiting the water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, highly adjustable photoluminescence and photoelectric transmission properties, CQDs hold the promising potential for the therapeutic of various diseases and the adjuvants for existing therapeutic methods [32–36].

Commonly, CQDs could be obtained by top-down strategies which cut and peel off from large carbon structure materials such as erosion or oxidation treatments [37–39]. Interestingly, similarly methods have also been used to prepare nano-scale Chinese herbal medicines [40]. Nevertheless, such intense methods would bring unpredictable changes or even damages to the efficacy components, impairing the medical efficiency. Another widely utilized method to prepare CQDs is known as bottom-up strategy, which involves the aggregation of small molecular precursors into carbon quantum dots by proper chemical reactions [41–44]. The bottom-up strategy demands high requirements for the resolution of the chemical structure. Usually, desirable monomers with reactive functional groups and the matched reaction mode are indispensable [45,46]. As precise and controlled synthesis strategies, bottom-up method could preserve the original structural composition while easily modify the structure with versatile structure and function, offering promising application prospects [47,48].

In this work, with curcumin as the original monomer, we have prepared series of functional curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots (termed as CCQD) via rational design and synthesis. Optimizing the carbonyl groups on curcumin which hold the possibility for condensation with amino groups (–NH2), we selected proper amino-rich monomers and realized the intermolecular cross-linking by Schiff base reaction. As polyphenols, original curcumin possessed poorly solubility in water, resulting to low bioavailability, poor permeability and high metabolic activity. Notably, the as-obtained CCQDs demonstrated superior water-solubility which benefited from the size effect of quantum dots. Besides, CCQDs also possessed advanced biocompatibility and fluorescent properties, which could efficiently enter cells while stain and label the cytoplasm. Moreover, compared with original curcumin, CCQDs induced adjustable and superior apoptosis towards tumor cells, which could also effectively inhibit the invasion and migration of tumor cells. The in vivo experiments in nude mice treated by CCQDs further evidenced the significant inhibition of tumor. This work provided a successful showcase of modification and functionalization of Chinses herbal and highlighted the curcumin as a promising candidate for cancer theranostics.

Curcumin and selected amino-rich molecules, ethanediamine, 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine, p-phenylenediamine and triaminoguanidine hydrochloride which contained multiple amino groups and versatile chemical constructions were used as the monomers to synthesize CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3 and CCQD-4, respectively. During the synthesis, groups on curcumin and the amino groups were linked through Schiff base condensation at the ratio of 1:1, 2:3, 1:1 and 2:3 by mole for CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4, respectively. For each reaction, the monomers were dissolved in DMF and reflux at 180 °C for 10 h to facilitate the dehydration condensation process. After reaction, the solution was evaporated thorough vacuum filtration at 80 ℃ and then washed by acetone. The samples were then kept under 150 °C in vacuum to remove the unreacted impurities before used. The products were obtained with the yield of 86 %.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of the samples were acquired on a Riguku D/Max-2500 PC X-ray diffractometer with Cu radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å). Zeta potential was acquired by a Nano Plus-3 zeta/nano particle analyzer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Hitachi UH4150 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) measurement was carried out using Zeiss Sigma 500. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a Talos F200X instrument.

The human lung cancer cell line A549 cells were cultured in PRMI 1640 (KGL1503-500, KeyGEN BioTECH, China) medium with 100 U/mL penicillin, 0.1 mg/mL streptomycin and 10 % fetal bovine serum under incubation conditions of 37 °C and 5 % CO2.

A total of 1*104 /mL cells were inoculated on 96-well plates (3365, Corning, China) and divided into five groups: curcumin group, CCQD-1 group, CCQD-2 group, CCQD-3 group, and CCQD-4 group. After incubation for 24 h, drugs were added in a gradient concentration series (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 80, and 160 μg/mL), respectively. Each group was set up with triplicate wells. Following 24 h incubation, the culture medium was removed and replaced with RPMI-1640 complete culture medium containing 10 μL CCK8 solution (KGA9305-1000, KeyGEN BioTECH, China). After 2 h incubation, the absorbance OD value at 450 nm of each well was measured using an enzyme marker.

The cell migration experiments were conducted using a permeable cell culture chamber (3413, Corning, China). The upper chamber was seeded with 1*106 /ml cells, while the lower chamber was filled with incomplete RPMI-1640 medium. After 24 h, the upper chamber was washed three times with 1 * PBS and then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 12 min. Subsequently, it was washed three times with 1 * PBS and stained with a crystal violet solution (500 μL) for 10 min. The cells on the membrane of the upper chamber were further cleaned three times using 1 * PBS and gently wiped off using a cotton swab. Finally, three fields of the upper chamber were captured and counted under an inverted microscope.

The cell invasion experiments were also conducted in a permeable chamber. Matrigel glue was thawed at 4 °C, mixed and diluted with serum-free medium at a ratio of 1:8. Then 80 μL of diluted Matrigel glue was added to the upper chamber. The chamber was incubated overnight at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2. The experimental procedure followed the protocol for cell migration experiments.

The AnnexinV-APC/7AAD kit (KGA1106-50, KeyGEN BioTECH, China) was utilized for the assessment of apoptosis ratio. The cells were harvested by EDTA-free pancreatic cell digestive fluid and centrifuged at 5000 g at 4 °C for 5 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed twice with pre-chilled 1 * PBS and centrifuged at 5000 g at 4 °C for 5 min. After discarding the supernatant, the cells were resuspended in binding buffer (500 μL) along with AnnexinV-APC and 7AAD reagents, separately. Following a dark incubation of 15 min at room temperature, Guava flow cytometry was employed to determine the apoptosis ratio.

The cells were co-cultured with curcumin, CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4 for 24 h. Subsequently, the nucleus was stained using hoechst33342 (KGA1804-10, KeyGEN BioTECH, China) and washed three times with 1 * PBS before being fixed with paraformaldehyde. Confocal microscopy was employed to capture images at specific excitation wavelengths: 420–480 nm, 613–654 nm, 552–575 nm, 650–750 nm, and 550–616 nm, respectively.

The animal experiments were conducted in accordance with national guidelines and approved by the Animal Research Committee of the Nanjing Ramda Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (SCXK2022-0038). Five-week-old female BALB/C nude mice were randomly allocated into six groups: curcumin control group, CCQD-1 group, CCQD-2 group, CCQD-3 group, CCQD-4 group, and cisplatin treatment group; each consisting of three mice. A total of 1 × 107 A549 cells were subcutaneously implanted into nude mice. When the tumor volume of mice reached ≥ 100 mm3, drug treatments were given according to the subgroup of nude mice. Tumor size was measured every three days. The tumor volume was calculated using the following formula: 0.52 * length * width2. Euthanasia was performed on day 21 after the initial injection and the implanted tumors were excised for further analysis.

Total RNA was extracted using the RNAiso Plus (9109, Thermo Scientific) following the manufacturer’s protocol. The quality and concentration of the extracted RNA were assessed using a Nanodrop 2000. High-quality RNA samples (RNA integrity number > 7.0) were subjected to mRNA sequencing using the Illumina platform. Libraries were prepared using the NEBNext Ultra II RNA Library Prep Kit and sequenced to achieve a minimum depth of 20 million reads per sample.

Sequencing data were processed and aligned to the Homo sapiens GRCh38 using R 4.2 software. Differential expression analysis was performed with DESeq2 package to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs), using an adjusted P-value threshold of < 0.05 and a log2|FC| > 2. Volcano plots and heatmaps were generated using ggplot2 and pheatmap packages to visualize the DEGs.

Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analyses were conducted using R ClusterProfiler package with the identified DEGs. Enriched terms were considered significant at a false discovery rate (FDR) < 0.05.
Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was performed using GSEA software against the MSigDB v7.0 database to assess the enrichment of gene sets.

A PPI network of DEGs was constructed using the STRING database, applying a confidence score threshold of 0.7. The network was visualized and analyzed using Cytoscape software to identify key hub proteins, applying the MCODE to determine network centrality.

All data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed using Student-t test with GraphPad Prism 9.0 software. Statistical differences between groups were established at a threshold of P < 0.05.

The carbon quantum dots in this work were prepared along the bottom-up route with curcumin and selected amino-rich molecules as monomers. Typically, carbonyl groups on curcumin and the amino groups were linked through Schiff base condensation, since the ketone and amine could undergo the nucleophilic addition reaction to form imine containing carbon–nitrogen double bond. The reactions were conducted under high temperature reflux to facilitate the dehydration condensation process. The dual carbonyl groups on curcumin and the amino groups facilitated the crosslinking polymerization and terminated by embedding of active site and caging effects, leading the formation of the carbon quantum dots [49–53]. Generally, ethanediamine, 2,4,6-triaminopyrimidine, p-phenylenediamine, and triaminoguanidine hydrochloride which contained multiple amino groups and versatile chemical constructions were used as the monomers to synthesize CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4, respectively (Fig. 1 a). According to the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. S1), the characteristic absorption peak of the excessively used curcumin could be observed at the center of 3500 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1. Around 3300 ∼ 3000 cm−1, the characteristic peaks could be attributed to the amides, while the weakening of curcumin carbonyl peaks were concurrently occurred. These phenomenons indicated the introduction of polyamino ligands and the formation of Schiff bases. Meanwhile, due to the diverse steric configuration and reactivity, the peak factors of each product were also different. For example, since the ethylenediamine with flexible structure were more reactive, the changes of the characteristic peaks CCQD-1 were more obvious. Because of high solubility and electronegativity of triaminoguanidine hydrochloride, the characteristic absorption peaks of –NH and –NH2 could be obviously observed in CCQD-4 at about 3300 cm−1. The elemental environment and chemical composition of each CCQDs were further studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments. As shown in Fig. S2, S3, S4, and S5, the major peaks at 200 to 600 eV were ascribed to C, O and N, respectively, which suggested pure composition of CCQDs. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum, the C1s spectrum could be further divided into three peaks at the binding energy around 284 and 286 eV, corresponding to C-C and C-N groups. The N1s spectrum were divided into 400.04 and 401.69 eV, indication the pyrroline N and graphite N [54]. As observed under high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the CCQDs were highly dispersed along the carbon support films (Fig. 1b). Even the nanoscale size leading to low contrast, the lattice fringes belong to CCQDs could be clearly observed, which were also suggested by the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of each CCQDs (Fig. 1c). Besides, as shown in Fig. 1c, the PXRD patterns of each CCQDs also demonstrated the broad diffraction peak at the 2θ around 25°, indicating the formation of graphitic carbon.

Benefiting from the essential attributes of quantum dots, the as-obtained CCQDs demonstrated promoted solubility and advanced optical properties. As shown in Fig. S6 and insets of 2b, the polyphenols nature of pure curcumin resulted into turbid solution even after ultrasonic treatment, indicating the very limited solubility in water which further hindered the bioavailability and related applications. On the contrary, all of the CCQDs could form very clear and stable solution systems in water. The surface charge was then evaluated through zeta potential. As shown in Fig. S7, CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4 possessed the zeta potential of −13.1 mV, −9.1 mV, −13.4 mV and 11.5 mV in aqueous solution, respectively. The high value revealed that the surface of each CCQDs was functionalized by ionically charged groups and indicated the promoted stability of the CCQDs solution systems. The optical properties were thoroughly analyzed by UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 2 a). As quantum dots, the CCQDs contained different absorption behavior and demonstrated new peak between 300 and 500 nm. Similar to general quantum dots, CCQDs all demonstrated wavelength-dependent behavior in fluorescence tests which was related to the size effects of quantum dots. Meanwhile, since the different monomers generated diversities such size, surface charge density, and elemental ratio, the CCQDs possessed diverse maximum emission intensity. As revealed in Fig. 2b, CCQD-1 was detected with a maximum emission intensity of 508 nm following the excitation at 401 nm, while the maximum emission intensity of CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4 were 563 nm, 586 nm, and 582 nm, respectively. Considering that fluorescence intensity was an important indicator for assessing the applications in biological area, such remarkable fluorescence properties were essential for the applications of bio-imaging or sensing.

The advanced solubility and biocompatibility of CCQDs endowed efficient performance in cellular uptake. Along with facilitated endocytic capability of A549 that benefiting from the biocompatibility of quantum dots, the promoted photoluminescence properties of CCQDs were further revealed. All CCQDs were detected by confocal microscopy predominantly around the nucleus and cytoplasm of A549 cells (Fig. 3 a), suggesting that A549 cells actively internalized CCQDs during the experiment. Furthermore, CCK-8 assay results demonstrated that all tested CCQDs exhibited enhanced cytotoxicity towards tumor cells (Fig. 3b). The proliferation capacity of A549 cells was observed to decrease progressively with increasing drug concentration, indicating a dose-dependent inhibitory effect of CCQDs on A549 cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 3b, the dose required to inhibit 50 % cell proliferation (IC50 values) at 24 h of pure curcumin, CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4 were 60 μM, 5 μM, 40 μM, 25 μM, and 40 μM, respectively. The significant intracellular accumulation behavior and the photoluminescence of CCQDs not only indicated the efficient absorbability, but also ensured the promising of capability of CCQDs for tumor theranostic.

Since original curcumin were suffering poorly solubility and low bioavailability, the applications as anti-tumor drugs were greatly hindered. Besides, due to the rapid metabolism, large amount of curcumin was required to obtain obvious effects. To this point, the curcumin derived CCQDs possessed superior water-solubility and biocompatibility, leading to promoted apoptosis of A549. As demonstrated in Fig. S8, compared to the control group, the original curcumin group exhibited an increased proportion of apoptosis with escalating concentrations, suggesting the potentials of curcumin as anti-tumor drugs. Specially, similar trends with enhanced performance were observed for CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4. Notably, at the working concentration of 10 μM, CCQD-1 significantly enhanced the apoptosis in A549 (Fig. 4 a, P < 0.001). Similarly, at a working concentration of 20 μM, CCQD-3 also induced a significant increase in apoptosis ratio in A549 (Fig. 4a, P < 0.001). Furthermore, with the working concentration of 40 μM, both CCQD-2 and CCQD-4 significantly elevated the apoptosis ratio in A549 (Fig. 4a, P < 0.001). Based on these phenomenons, the superior apoptotic-inducing potentials of CCQDs in A549 compared to the curcumin group were clearly evident.

Moreover, the CCQDs also generated advanced inhibitory effects on the migration and invasion of A549. As shown in Fig. 4b, 10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, and 80 μM were chosen as the working concentrations for co-culturing with A549 to assess the inhibitory effects. In contrast to the results on the apoptosis of A549, compare to the natural condition (NC) groups, there were no significant differences observed in the migration (Fig. 4b and S9, P > 0.05) and invasion (Fig. 4c and S10, P > 0.05) abilities of the original curcumin group with increasing drug concentration. Expectedly, all of the CCQDs could significantly suppress the migration and invasion of A549. At the working concentration of 10 μM for CCQD-1, there was a significant decrease in A549 migration (Fig. 4b, P < 0.001) and invasion (Fig. 4b, P < 0.001). Similarly, the migration (Fig. 4b, P < 0.001) and invasion (Fig. 4c, P < 0.001) abilities of cells were significantly inhibited when the working concentration of CCQD-3 was 20 μM. Additionally, for CCQD-2 and CCQD-4, there were significant reductions in A549 migration (Fig. 4c, P < 0.001) and invasion (Fig. 4c, P < 0.001) at the working concentration of 40 μM. These results indicated that the construction of curcumin to CCQDs could lead to superior inhibition abilities on A549 migration and invasion compared to curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo performance to inhibit tumor growth
ce:section.ce:para: Inherit from the original curcumin as the traditional Chinese herbal, CCQDs preserved considerable biosafety and practical utility even for in vivo therapy. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the results of the transplanted tumor model in nude mice demonstrated a significant inhibition of tumor in CCQD-1, CCQD-2, CCQD-3, and CCQD-4 compared to the original curcumin group. Specially, CCQD-1 exhibited the weakest inhibitory effect on tumor volume (Fig. 5b, P < 0.001), followed by CCQD-2 (Fig. 5b, P < 0.001) and CCQD-4 (Fig. 5b, P < 0.001). Conversely, CCQD-3 displayed the strongest inhibition on tumor volume (Fig. 5b, P < 0.001). The same outcomes were observed in terms of tumor weights. CCQD-1 exhibited the lowest efficacy in reducing tumor weight in nude mice (Fig. 5c, P < 0.001), followed by CCQD-2 (Fig. 5c, P < 0.001) and CCQD-4 (Fig. 5c, P < 0.001), while CCQD-3 demonstrated the highest potency in reducing tumor weight in nude mice (Fig. 5c, P < 0.001), demonstrating the powerful abilities of the four drugs to inhibit tumor. It should be noted that even compared with cisplatin which is one of most used for clinical medication, the CCQDs especially CCQD-3 demonstrated comparable performance. In addition, as the derivatives of Chinese herbal, CCQDs confirmed considerable biosafety and biocompatibility. The main organs of the mice treated with CCQDs were collected and the studied by H&E staining. As shown in Fig. 5d, none pathological damage could be found.

The experiments in vivo in nude mice further revealed that the treatment with CCQDs could significantly inhibit the tumors. Previously, curcumin has been explored with significant properties such as antibacterial, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory functions. For example, it has been reported that curcumin could clear the reactive oxygen species (ROS) and enhance the antioxidant capacity of cells by activating the NRF2 pathway, promoting the healing of infected diabetic wounds [55]. Researchers have also found that the loading of curcumin could inhibit the inflammatory cascade of glial/macrophages and neurotoxic astrocytes, protect against inflammation-induced apoptosis during nerve regeneration, thus improving motor function and hindlimb coordination in a mouse model of traumatic spinal cord injury [56,57]. Curcumin also holds the capability for the regulation of lipid metabolism [58] and broad-spectrum antibacterial scenarios [53]. Even so, the poor solubility and short half-life of curcumin still limit its bioavailability and therapeutic effectiveness. It is vital to develop suitable vectors to enhance availability and facilitate efficient delivery of curcumin. The results in this work developed the promising potentials of curcumin-based drugs as a promising candidate for cancer theranostics, which further extended the application of curcumin and derivatives. More importantly, by taking advantage of CQDs, the discovery in this work offered new strategies to modify and overcome the obstacles of curcumin. It should be noted that most traditional Chinese herbal medicines, especially those have shown clear anti-tumor effects, possess great industrial production potential and application value. Hence, we believe that by combining with more areas such as chemistry and material approaches could advance the natural bioactivities, endow more functionalities and identify stronger drug treatments.

To have a deep insight of the anti-tumor mechanism of CCQDs, we further performed the RNA sequencing studies for each as-designed drug. According to the acquisition of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and functional enrichment analysis (Fig. 6 a and 6b), the DEGs were visually represented using generated heatmaps and volcano diagrams. A total of 526 up-regulated genes and 417 down-regulated genes were obtained. GO analysis was then conducted to uncover insights into biological processes (BP), cellular components (CC), and molecular functions (MF). The results were summarized in Fig. S11 and highlight the top 40 GO items. The DEGs identified exhibited significant enrichment in the regulation of localization, transport, positive regulation of signaling, and cell communication. This set of genes exerted a positive influence on extracellular region, extracellular space, and endoplasmic reticulum, and it was also related to cation transmembrane transport activity, inorganic cation transmembrane transporter activity, signaling receptor regulator activity, and receptor ligand activity. Moreover, the set of DEGs played a role in cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, chemokine signaling pathway, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, and circadian entrainment (Fig. S12). The Hub genes identification and functional analysis were then conducted. The PPI network was constructed based on the top 100 DEGs, and the most prominent 22 genes were further screened utilizing the MCODE (Fig. 6c). This 22-genes set enrich in chemokine signaling pathway, IL-17 signaling pathway, T cell receptor signaling pathway, TNF signaling pathway, and natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity (Fig. S13).

In physiological conditions, IL-17 serves as a critical mediator in maintaining bodily homeostasis and health [59]. However, under pathological conditions, this balance is disrupted, and the IL-17 signaling pathway becomes intricately involved in tumor initiation, proliferation, metastasis, and drug resistance. IL-17 is a highly versatile pro-inflammatory cytokine that promotes chemokine production through its interaction with target tumor cells, playing an essential role in the development and progression of most cancers [60]. Primarily expressed by CD4 + T cells, IL-17 can also be produced by CD8 + T cells and natural killer cells during inflammatory responses [61–63]. Conversely, activation of CD4 + and CD8 + T cell receptors leads to the production of IL-17. IL-17 exerts its effects by binding to its ligand molecules and their receptors, thereby stimulating and activating downstream signaling pathways such as TNF receptor-associated factor (TRAF) and E3 ligase-mediated RNA binding [64,65], which subsequently induces transcription and activation of downstream oncogenes. It is hypothesized that CCQDs may play significant regulatory roles in the chemokine signaling pathway, T cell receptor pathway, and TNF pathway by participating in the IL-17 signaling pathway, providing a novel direction for the clinical development of innovative drugs for tumor therapy.

In this work, aiming at the natural products curcumin, we fabricated the curcumin-derived carbon quantum dots by combining with the sophisticated chemistry methods. Expectedly, these rationally designed CCQDs well inherited the advantages of quantum dots including size effect, fluorescence with the biological function of curcumin. Compared with the original curcumin, the CCQDs not only demonstrated superior bioavailability and permeability, but also possessed considerable biocompatibility with fluorescent properties. During in vitro experiments, CCQDs could efficiently enter cells while stain and label the cytoplasm. Besides, CCQDs was able to induce superior apoptosis towards tumor cells, while effectively inhibited the invasion and migration of tumor cells. The in vivo experiments in nude mice further revealed that the treatment with CCQDs could significantly inhibit the tumors. These results developed the promising potentials of curcumin-based drugs as a promising candidate for cancer theranostics. Moreover, the discovery in this work offered new strategies to modify Chinses herbal and develop bioactive nanomaterials. It should be noted that most traditional Chinese herbal medicines, especially those have shown clear anti-tumor effects, possess great industrial production potential and application value. Hence, we believe that by combining with more areas such as chemistry and material approaches could advance the natural bioactivities, endow more functionalities and identify stronger drug treatments.

Hongjie Chen: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wen Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hong Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
